skill.name: ddd-tactical-patterns
skill.description: Apply DDD tactical patterns in code using entities, value objects, aggregates, repositories, and domain events with explicit invariants.
skill.summary: Apply DDD tactical patterns in code using entities, value objects, aggregates, repositories, and domain events with explicit invariants.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The 'external_commands' detections were Markdown backticks around code examples (e.g., `OrderSubmitted`), not actual shell execution. The 'blocker' findings for weak cryptography and system reconnaissance were triggered by documentation text, not executable code. This is a safe educational skill containing only DDD pattern guidance and TypeScript examples.

content.user_title: Apply DDD Tactical Patterns to Your Code
content.value_statement: Struggling to translate domain rules into clean code structures? This skill guides you through DDD tactical patterns including entities, value objects, aggregates, repositories, and domain events.
actual_capabilities: Identify invariants and design aggregates around them
Model immutable value objects with validation
Keep domain behavior in domain objects, not controllers
Emit domain events for meaningful state transitions
Define repository contracts at aggregate boundaries
limitations: Does not define deployment architecture or infrastructure decisions
Does not choose databases or transport protocols
Requires pairing with testing patterns for invariant coverage
Assumes basic understanding of object-oriented programming

use_cases.title: Refactor Anemic Domain Models
use_cases.description: Transform data-only classes into behavior-rich domain objects that enforce business rules.
use_cases.target_user: Backend developers working with legacy anemic models

use_cases.title: Design New Aggregates
use_cases.description: Structure new domain code with proper aggregate boundaries and invariant enforcement.
use_cases.target_user: Software architects designing new bounded contexts

use_cases.title: Implement Domain Events
use_cases.description: Add event-driven communication between aggregates using past-tense domain events.
use_cases.target_user: Developers building event-sourced or CQRS systems

prompt_templates.title: Basic Aggregate Design
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to model an Order aggregate. Help me identify the invariants, design the entity with behavior methods, and define appropriate value objects for this domain.

prompt_templates.title: Value Object Modeling
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an immutable value object for Money that includes currency and amount. Include validation at construction and equality by value.

prompt_templates.title: Repository Contract Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a repository interface for the Order aggregate that exposes domain-friendly query methods without leaking ORM implementation details.

prompt_templates.title: Domain Event Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me define domain events for Order state transitions. Include past-tense naming, minimal payloads, and schema versioning strategy.

output_examples.input: Model an Order aggregate with submit and cancel operations
output_examples.output: class Order with private status field, submit() method that validates items count and enforces single-transition invariant, and cancel() method with appropriate guard conditions. Includes domain event emission for OrderSubmitted and OrderCancelled.

output_examples.input: Create a value object for Email address
output_examples.output: Immutable Email class with regex validation in constructor, equality by value comparison, and toString() method. Rejects invalid formats at construction time.

best_practices: Identify invariants first, then design aggregate boundaries around them
Keep value objects immutable with validation at construction time
Name domain events in past tense with minimal, stable payloads
anti_patterns: Cross-aggregate synchronous consistency rules that violate transaction boundaries
Leaking ORM entities into the domain layer
Controllers containing domain business logic instead of domain objects

faq.question: When should I use aggregates versus simple entities?
faq.answer: Use aggregates when you have invariants that must be enforced transactionally. An aggregate root is the entry point that ensures consistency across all objects within its boundary.

faq.question: Should value objects be mutable?
faq.answer: No. Value objects should be immutable by default. Any modification creates a new instance. This ensures thread safety and simplifies reasoning about state.

faq.question: How do I handle cross-aggregate communication?
faq.answer: Use domain events for eventual consistency between aggregates. Avoid synchronous calls that couple aggregates and break transaction boundaries.

faq.question: What belongs in a repository interface?
faq.answer: Repository interfaces should expose domain-friendly query methods and only persist or load aggregate roots. They should not leak ORM-specific implementation details.

faq.question: How do I know if I need domain events?
faq.answer: Use domain events when other parts of the system need to react to state changes without tight coupling. They are essential for audit trails, notifications, and CQRS patterns.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for microservices architecture?
faq.answer: Yes. DDD tactical patterns work well with microservices. Each service can have its own aggregates, and domain events can facilitate inter-service communication.
